title: Pane di Altamura
subtitle: Pugliese durum semolina loaf
excerpt: The bread of Altamura in Puglia, made entirely with durum wheat semolina and shaped into the traditional U-shape or round. The golden crumb is dense and nutty, the crust thick and almost crackerlike — a loaf that improves on the second day.
sourceNotes: Pane di Altamura has DOP (Protected Designation of Origin) status in Italy and must be made with durum wheat from the Murgia plateau to carry the name commercially. The method described here is based on traditional Pugliese home-baking practice documented in Italian regional food literature and agricultural extension material. Semolina rimacinata (twice-milled) is the domestic substitute for the traditional molino-ground flour.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely before freezing. Freeze whole or sliced. Thaw at room temperature overnight or warm in the oven at 180°C for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be retarded overnight in the fridge after shaping. Remove and leave at room temperature for 1 hour before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, preheated for at least 45 minutes with the baking stone or steel. Fan at 210°C.
baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 15 minutes to develop the thick crust

glossaryTerms.definition: Twice-milled durum wheat semolina, finer than coarse semolina but coarser than plain flour. The standard flour for pane di Altamura.
A rest period after mixing flour and water, before adding salt and yeast, that allows gluten to begin developing without kneading. Particularly useful with high-protein flours like semolina.

content.text: Durum wheat makes a bread that nothing else quite replicates: a golden crumb, almost sandy in texture but cohesive, with a crust that is thick and hard and improves with keeping.

is the flour to find: it is twice-milled durum, fine enough for bread but still coarser than plain flour. Coarse semolina (polenta-grind) does not work for this recipe — it absorbs water differently and produces a gritty crumb.
Combine the semolina and 300 ml of the lukewarm water in a large bowl. Mix until no dry flour remains, then cover and rest for 30 minutes. This
step allows the coarse grain to hydrate fully and gives the gluten a head start, which matters with durum's tough protein network.

Dissolve the yeast in the remaining 25 ml of water and add to the dough along with the salt and olive oil. Knead by hand for 12–15 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic. Durum dough is stiffer than plain-flour bread dough at the same hydration; it should feel firm but pliable, not sticky.

Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave at room temperature for 2 hours until the dough has doubled. Semolina dough ferments a little more slowly than white flour bread; give it the full time.

Turn the dough onto an unfloured surface and shape into a tight round or the traditional U-shape (cappello di monaco). For the U-shape: roll the dough into a long oval, fold one third over the centre, then fold the other third over that, and curve the ends to form a crescent. Place on a semolina-dusted baking sheet, cover, and proof for 90 minutes. The loaf should have clearly puffed when you look at it from the side.

Preheat the oven to 230°C conventional with a baking stone on the middle shelf and a water tray on the floor. The oven needs at least 45 minutes to reach a genuine 230°C — domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer confirms it. Score the top of the loaf with a sharp blade in a single long slash or a grid pattern. Slide onto the hot stone and bake for 15 minutes with the water tray in place, then remove the tray and bake for a further 25–30 minutes until the crust is deep amber and sounds hollow when tapped. An internal probe reading of 96°C confirms it is done.
